320 Center Street
Property and Disclosure Briefing

320 Center Street
Healdsburg

Disclosure history, permits and entitlements, and investor considerations for a proposed restaurant use
Prepared for Courtland Palmer  |  Honmono Project  |  July 16, 2026  |  Confidential
1  |  Executive Summary

The building is clean.
The entitlement is not.

The physical asset presents well: an unusually clean disclosure record, a comprehensive 2021 to 2025 renovation, and a favorable seismic profile. But the single most important fact in the document set is unfavorable. The existing Conditional Use Permit authorizes a wine tasting room and wine bar, not a restaurant.

Every assurance about the permit surviving the sale applies only if the use continues unchanged. The City's own email says a change in the scope of operation is exactly the circumstance in which the run-with-the-land protection falls away. A 12-seat omakase restaurant is a change in scope. Honmono should underwrite this project on the assumption that a new or amended CUP is required. (Judgment, strongly supported by the documents.)

Property
320 Center St, with 124-1 and 124-2 Plaza St and 126 Plaza St, Healdsburg, CA 95448
APN
002-194-006
Sellers
SJM Enterprises LLC and the Bucher Trust (signed Jan 24, 2026; buyers acknowledged Jun 5, 2026)
Prior use
Everett Vineyards Tasting Room; original 2010 CUP applicant DeLoach Vineyards
Zoning context
Plaza Retail Zoning District, Downtown Commercial designation
Sources
CSPQ (C.A.R. 6/25), Improvements Disclosures (Jan 2026), Earthquake Risk Disclosure (2022 ed.), CUP Resolutions 2010-12 and 2014-11, City email Jun 3 to 4, 2026

3  |  Material Findings

Five findings that shape the restaurant thesis

Finding 01
Hard 10:00 pm close
No operation past 10:00 pm, including special events; no amplified music audible outside. A late second seating must fit the window or the condition must be renegotiated. (Certain, CUP 2014-08 Cond. 3, 5.)
Finding 02
Entrance locked to Center St
City confirmed in writing: no separate Plaza Street entrance, entrance must remain on Center Street, and the building cannot be divided into separate tasting rooms. (Certain.)
Finding 03
Capacity fees are lurking
The City must run a water and sewer use analysis at the next building permit, with purchase of added capacity if credits fall short. A commercial kitchen is the demand case the condition was written for. (Certain / Likely.)
Finding 04
The parklet is not entitled
No conditional use permit exists for the Plaza Street parklet; it is a Public Works administrative matter. Underwrite outdoor covers at zero. (Certain.)
Finding 05
Family-held fractional title
Every instrument needs both the trust and the LLC signature blocks; confirm the incoming buyer takes 100 percent of fee title at close. (Certain, CSPQ Sec. 15.)
4  |  Property Overview

A corner of the Healdsburg Plaza

320 Center Street is a two-story mixed-use building at the corner of Center and Plaza Streets, fronting the historic Healdsburg Plaza. The disclosure package treats it as one ownership across four spaces: the ground-floor commercial space at 320 Center (the former tasting room), the adjoining 126 Plaza Street space added by the 2014 CUP expansion, and two upstairs residential apartments at 124 Plaza Street.

The building operated for roughly fifteen years as a City-authorized wine tasting room under two successive planning approvals, was substantially renovated by the current owners, and went into contract in mid-2026. Buyer side: the Cadenhead / Caska group, represented by Graham Sarasy of Healdsburg Sotheby's; listing side: Sonoma Realty Group. Built before 1978; exact year built is not stated in any reviewed document.

5  |  Seller Property Questionnaire

What the sellers affirmed

The CSPQ (C.A.R. Form 6/25, five pages) is the sellers' sworn statement of known material facts; no Transfer Disclosure Statement is required for this commercial sale. The Yes answers:

ItemDisclosureSo what
5A / 5B / 5DSurveys, plans and engineering documents exist; permits and approvals including CUPs; other reports and inspections. Sellers must deliver them to the buyer.Confirm the 5A engineering and survey files actually land in the data room.
6ABuilt before January 1, 1994; plumbing fixtures compliant with Civil Code 1101.3.Water-conserving fixture compliance already addressed, consistent with the renovation.
6BStandard water heater of 120 gallons or less; bracing certified at close.Matches the earthquake form (braced: Yes).
15AAnother entity on title: SJM Enterprises LLC at 36 percent, children of the 64 percent Bucher Trust owners. The only handwritten explanation on the form.Two signatories for everything; family governance behind every consent.
18ABuilt before 1978; no lead-paint renovations started or completed.Lead-safe work practices required during any build-out demolition.
18BContains single or two-unit residential structures; smoke detectors certified at close.The two upstairs apartments ride along with the asset.
6  |  Seller Property Questionnaire

What the sellers denied, and one inconsistency

The sellers answered No to every condition and risk question in Sections 7 through 17 and 19 through 21:

No defects in any building system: HVAC, electrical, plumbing, sewer, roof, foundation, drainage, windows, walls, floors
No water intrusion, standing water, flooding, mold, mildew or fungus, past or present
No environmental hazards: asbestos, formaldehyde, radon, storage tanks, contaminated soil or water
No fill, settling, slippage or soil problems; no fire, earthquake, flood or landslide damage
No lawsuits, liens, notices of default, bankruptcies, easement or boundary disputes, PACE assessments
No insurance claims or disaster relief history; no leased systems; no septic
No eminent domain, rent control, moratoria, zoning violations, protected habitat or historic designation
No rental or service agreements, income statements or tenant estoppels disclosed
One inconsistency worth a written correction. Section 7A (awareness of alterations, improvements or material repairs) is checked No, yet the same package discloses seventeen renovation projects completed 2021 to 2025, and the 7A sub-questions are answered in a way that presumes the work happened. No concealment concern, but ask the sellers to correct or explain 7A in writing before close. Also notable: 17.I answers No to historic designation, though downtown design review can still reach facade and signage changes.
7  |  Improvements Disclosure

A four-year, seventeen-project renovation

Between October 2021 and December 2025 the owners renovated all four spaces: new subflooring and hardwood after a perimeter foundation inspection, new HVAC in 320 and 126, full interior and exterior repaint, wallpaper and wainscoting, built-in cabinets, remodeled kitchen area and bathrooms, a new commercial dishwasher and water heater in 320, IT, speaker and security wiring, refinished stairwells, new exterior overhangs, and a City-permitted redwood parklet along Plaza Street.

Two cautions. What matters most to Honmono is what was not done: no Type I hood, grease interceptor, gas or electrical service upgrade, or structural work appears anywhere in the disclosure, so the omakase kitchen remains a full scope item on top of a just-finished interior. (Likely, from absence.) And the dish infrastructure in the photos is tasting-room scale: a head start on a health-permit floor plan, not a restaurant back-of-house. (Judgment.)

8  |  Earthquake Risk Disclosure

Clean on every structural risk question

Commercial Property Earthquake Risk Disclosure Report (2022 Edition), executed by both sellers January 24, 2026:

Water heaters braced, strapped or anchored: Yes
Precast, tilt-up or masonry walls with wood-frame floors or roof: Doesn't Apply
Unreinforced masonry: Doesn't Apply (consistent with CSPQ 6E; no Earthquake Safety Guide delivery triggered)
Pre-1980 concrete, pre-1995 steel moment frame, soft-story or open-front design: Doesn't Apply in each case
Exterior cladding and signage adequately anchored: Yes
Alquist-Priolo Fault Zone: No  ·  Seismic Hazard Zone (liquefaction or landslide): No
Two gaps: the Year Built field was left blank, and this is a self-assessment rather than an engineer's report. For a pre-1978 building taking on assembly occupancy, a structural engineer's letter during due diligence is cheap insurance, and these answers give the engineer a favorable starting point. (Disclosure content Certain; recommendation Judgment.)
9  |  Permits and Entitlements

Two planning approvals, one operative permit

ApprovalScopeKey conditions
UP 10-5
Resolution 2010-12
Adopted Apr 27, 2010 (4-0)		 Wine tasting facility and bar at 320 Center Street (DeLoach Vineyards), replacing a prior tasting facility at the same location. Wine retail and tasting 10:30 am to 5:00 pm; wines by the glass Thu to Sun 5:00 to 10:00 pm; closed by 10:00 pm; no amplified music; ABC license required; building permit before any remodel with ADA compliance; water and sewer use analysis at the next building permit, with purchase of additional capacity if credits are inadequate.
CUP 2014-08
Resolution 2014-11
Adopted Jul 22, 2014		 Expansion to an approx. 1,160 sf wine tasting room, wine bar and special events space spanning 320 Center and 126 Plaza. CEQA Sec. 15303 exemption. Amended and restated conditions govern today. Wine for retail sale and tasting only, 10:00 am to 10:00 pm; no operation past 10:00 pm including special events; no amplified music audible outside; windows kept transparent; no right-of-way use without written approval; ABC license before operations; ADA path of travel between 320 and 126; tenant improvement permit with ADA upgrades; eliminate the Plaza St address and direct customers to the Center St entrance; revocable on violation.
The listing brokerage's CUP overview asserts the permit is site-based, does not terminate on sale, and may allow a new owner to continue the tasting room use without new discretionary approvals. It also disclaims any warranty on transferability and directs every buyer and tenant to verify with the City. Treat it as sell-side framing of a real but narrow fact: the entitlement survives the sale only inside its existing scope.
10  |  City Confirmation

Three load-bearing statements from the City

Buyer broker Graham Sarasy asked the City to confirm CUP transferability for his clients, in contract to purchase the building. Jeff Fisher, Assistant Planner, City of Healdsburg, replied June 4, 2026:

1
"Use permits run with the land. Change of ownership or operator has no effect on the use permit, unless there is a planned expansion or change in the scope of the operation." The qualifier is the entire ballgame for Honmono. (Certain.)
2
No separate entrance on Plaza Street. The entrance must remain on Center Street, and the building cannot be divided into separate tasting rooms. This forecloses any two-concept split of 320 and 126 under the existing permit. (Certain.)
3
No conditional use permit exists for the parklet; it is regulated by Public Works. The buyer's broker could not locate a parklet permit in the building file; the City's reply did not produce one. Chase the actual permit through Public Works. (Certain that no CUP exists.)
11  |  Key Issues for a Restaurant Use

Ranked by potential impact on Honmono

High impact
Entitlement does not cover a restaurant
Condition 3 limits the business to wine for retail sale and tasting only. Assume a new or amended CUP before the Planning Commission. The findings that supported the tasting room are arguably stronger for a nationally significant restaurant, and the City has twice approved alcohol uses at this corner; favored but not automatic. (Judgment.)
Medium-high
Operating condition mismatches
The 10:00 pm hard stop constrains a two-seating format; an 8:30 pm seating running two-plus hours breaches it. Window transparency requirements interact with an intimate 12-seat room. Occupancy limits are a documentation item, not a constraint.
Medium
Physical plant and permitting costs
Kitchen build-out (hood, grease, gas, electrical) is undisclosed territory; the water and sewer capacity analysis is certain to occur at the next permit; ADA path-of-travel obligations attach to any tenant improvement; pre-1978 lead-safe practices; commission a structural letter.
Medium
Title and counterparty complexity
The 64/36 trust-and-LLC split means two signatures for everything. If Honmono leases from the incoming buyers, confirm the lessor holds 100 percent of fee title post-close.
Low-medium
Parklet impermanence
Recently built and attractive, but permitted only administratively through Public Works. Value it at zero; treat continued use as upside secured through a license in the operator's own name.
Low
Housekeeping
CSPQ 7A correction; Year Built from county records; 5A document delivery; confirm the residential units are vacant or obtain estoppels (Section 19 answered No, which likely means vacant).
12  |  What Is Clean

The affirmative case

No known defects in any system. No water, mold, environmental, soil or damage history. No litigation, liens, disputes or assessments. No unpermitted work asserted anywhere in the package. A favorable seismic profile: wood frame, no URM or soft-story typology, outside mapped fault and hazard zones.

Fifteen-plus years of continuous, City-sanctioned alcohol service at this corner, with two Planning Commission approvals and findings that a replacement licensed use does not over-concentrate the Plaza. And a 2021 to 2025 renovation delivering new HVAC, flooring, finishes, exterior paint and weather protection, IT and security infrastructure, and upstairs units in rentable condition. As a shell for a high-end build-out, the asset presents well; the work is in the entitlement, not the walls.

13  |  Recommended Due Diligence

Six actions, in order

1
Pre-application meeting with Healdsburg Planning to confirm the entitlement path (CUP amendment vs. new CUP), realistic hearing timeline, and the City's appetite on hours. Do this before finalizing lease economics; the answer sets the critical path.
2
Obtain the water and sewer capacity methodology, fee schedule and the property's existing credits, and put a number in the model rather than the surprise column.
3
Pull the full permit history for 320 Center, 124 Plaza and 126 Plaza to verify the 2021 to 2025 work was permitted and finaled, and locate the parklet permit through Public Works.
4
Commission a structural engineer's letter addressing assembly occupancy in a pre-1978 wood-frame building; confirm year built from county records.
5
Sequence the ABC licensing strategy: a bona fide eating place license (Type 41 or 47) is a different instrument from the wine-focused license the tasting room held, with its own timeline against the CUP process.
6
Resolve the paper items: CSPQ 7A correction, 5A document delivery, residential unit vacancy or estoppels, and post-close title confirmation from the incoming ownership group.
Bottom Line
A well-documented, well-renovated building with a genuinely clean disclosure history and the wrong entitlement for the intended use. The project risk lives almost entirely at the Planning counter, and it should be priced and scheduled accordingly.
320 Center Street, Healdsburg  |  Honmono Project  |  Prepared July 16, 2026  |  Confidential
